coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots as a green sorbent for micro-solid phase extraction of triazole fungicide residues in edible fungi samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kachangoon, Rawikan
Santaladchaiyakit, Yanawath
Prasitnok, Orrasa
Vichapong, Jitlada
coredata.dc:description: Fabrication of hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots was investigated to extract triazoles prior to HPLC-DAD quantitation. The melamine sponge was simply incorporated with carbon dots via thermal treatment under facile conditions. The optimum conditions were melamine sponge (MeS) (1 × 1 × 1 cm3), 10 mL of standard solution, 0.04 M citric acid, functionalized at 250 °C for 5 h, and the analytes was then eluted from as-prepared material using acetonitrile (200 µL). Operating the best conditions, wide linearity was gained with R2 ≥ 0.99, for all analytes. Detection limits established varied between 3–10 μg L−1. Such approached method demonstrates excellent applicability for accurately and efficiently determining triazole fungicides in edible fungi samples, and a good recovery was also obtained. In addition, the desirable method validation and cost effective, not only its potential for reuse, but also provides it a better alternative for matrix purification. The appraisal estimated by two methods, confirmed its superior environmental friendliness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fungicides
Carbon dots
Sample preconcentration
Micro-SPE
Edible fungi

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots
Characterization of superhydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots
Reusability and selectivity of the sorbent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis and micro-SPE application of carbon dot embedded melamine sponge.

FE-SEM of (a) bare MeS, (b) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge, (c) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after adsorption with triazole fungicides (100 μg L−1 of each) and (d) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after desorption process.

XRD pattern of (a) bare MeS, (b) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after adsorption with triazole fungicides (100 μg L−1 of each) and (c) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after desorption process.

FTIR spectra of (a) bare MeS, (b) CA coated MeS, (c) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after adsorption with triazole fungicides (100 μg L−1 of each) and (d) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge after desorption process.
BET isotherm without modification (a) and with modification (b).
The mechanism for the functionalization of CD-MeS.
Comparison of water contact angle on modified and unmodified sponge surfaces.
TGA curves of (a)bare melamine sponge (MeS) and (b) carbon dot embedded melamine sponge (MeS-CD).
Chemical stability of the as-prepared sorbent.

row.entry: RSD (%, 100 μg L−1 of each triazole)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparisons of the proposed method with other sample preparation methods for the quantitation of triazole fungicides.

row.entry: Environmental water, soybean milk, fruit juice and alcoholic beverage samples
Environmental water, honey and fruit juice samples

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *GO-PmAP: Graphene oxide-poly 3-aminophenol, Azo-PP/PDMS: Azo-linked porous organic polymers/polydimethylsiloxane coated stir bar, In situ SMMH: In situ self-assembled coating of surfactant-mixed metal hydroxide on Fe3O4@SiO2 magnetic composite, CAs: Carbon aerogels, CA-CD- MeS: Citric acid- carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method, SPE: Solid phase extraction, MSPE: Magnetic solid phase extraction, SBSE: Stir bar sportive extraction, dSPE: Dispersive solid phase extraction, PT-SPE: Pipette-tip solid-phase extraction, HPLC-UV: High performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet detector, HPLC-DAD: High performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector, HPLC-MS/MS: High performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry, GC-FID: Gas chromatography-flame ionization detector.

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots as a green sorbent for micro-solid phase extraction of triazole fungicide residues in edible fungi samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Multidisciplinary Research Unit of Pure and Applied Chemistry (MRUPAC)
Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellent for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots was investigated to extract triazoles prior to HPLC-DAD quantitation. The melamine sponge was simply incorporated with carbon dots via thermal treatment under facile conditions. The optimum conditions were melamine sponge (MeS) (1 × 1 × 1 cm3), 10 mL of standard solution, 0.04 M citric acid, functionalized at 250 °C for 5 h, and the analytes was then eluted from as-prepared material using acetonitrile (200 µL). Operating the best conditions, wide linearity was gained with R2 ≥ 0.99, for all analytes. Detection limits established varied between 3–10 μg L−1. Such approached method demonstrates excellent applicability for accurately and efficiently determining triazole fungicides in edible fungi samples, and a good recovery was also obtained. In addition, the desirable method validation and cost effective, not only its potential for reuse, but also provides it a better alternative for matrix purification. The appraisal estimated by two methods, confirmed its superior environmental friendliness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Triazole fungicides (TFs) are ordinarily utilized in cultivation to preclude and control fungal during the planting and postharvest storage of crops. Due to the unique property, they highly bioactive with a good control effect on most harmful pathogenic bacteria [1]. The attention of the use of triazole fungicides is as a result of their exceptional antifungal activity, relatively low resistance risk and their long-term stability in soil and water. Their response mode is based on the inhibition of the fungal ergosterol biosynthetic pathway and inhibition of steroid demethylation [2]. Because their photochemical stability and low biodegradability, as a result they could effortlessly distribute in various cultivation manufactures and transit in environment [3]. To protect the environment and human health because of their strong systemic action and persistence [4], many countries have made strict regulations on the limit of triazole residues in foodstuffs. The European Union (EU) [5] have set the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of triazole fungicides in fungi are 0.01mg kg –1. Due to their long stability and toxicity even at low concentrations, consequently, it is critical to establish rapid and accurate residue trace methods for triazole fungicides.

However, a preliminary separation and preconcentration procedure are required to remove any matrix components, improve sensitivity and accuracy results before instrumental detection [6]. Solid-phase extraction (SPE) [7,8] has been widely used for carrying out preparation and enrichment of the target compounds. The target analytes adsorbed in solid sorbent material by some interaction such as electrostatic interaction, after that the analytes were eluted using a small amount of an effective solvent [9]. However, SPE requires large volumes of sample and extraction solvent, and time intensive. Microextraction such as micro-SPE, has become a vital choice for sustainability of sample preparation as it requires less amounts of reagents and materials. Small sizes of sorbent (<5 µm) and less volume of sample solution were used in micro-SPE method [10]. In general, adsorbent has a crucial function in SPE-based methods because it determines the large specific surface area, and highly porous structure. Moreover, this method can be easily and cheaply fabricated in the laboratory, compared to conventional method. Consequently, inquiry for finding new solid phase sorbent material is a significant subject.

Melamine sponge (MeS) is a commercially available 3D material with unique wettability, a porous structure, and a high specific surface area [11]. Although these advantages, however, the highest drawback of melamine sponges is their hydrophilicity [12]. In order to enhance the adsorption capacity and selectivity of the adsorbent, melamine sponges have been modified to exhibit a hydrophobic behavior [13] for various applications [14] by different materials, including metal–organic frameworks [15], Urea-Formaldehyde Co-Oligomers [16], hydrophilic MIL-68(Al)/Chitosan [14], β-cyclodextrin-graphene oxide [17], MIL-101(Cr)/MWCNTs [18] and layered double hydroxide [19].

Carbon-based materials have been utilized in the extraction field for a long time due to their outstanding characteristics. High particular surface area, effective pore sizes, durability to acids and elements, adjustable surface physical structure, and affordability are some of the highlighted benefits [20]. Carbon dots [21] are new zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm. Some studies confirmed that carbon dots have some polar functional groups, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, lactone, acid anhydride, pyrone and so on [22]. Up to now, there has been an increasingly of studied about the synthesis and application of these materials owing to their unique properties such as high solubility, ease of functionalization, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [23]. The hyphenation of melamine sponge functionalized with carbon nanodots using citric acid as precursor coupled to gas chromatography-mass spectrometry system has been studied for different analytes [24]. This reported method required time-consuming for functionalization processes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots, employing a single-step via a thermal treatment, is utilized successfully in a preconcentration method for triazole fungicide residues in fungi samples prior to HPLC detection.

In the present study, to exhibit a hydrophobic of MeS and investigate as an efficient sorbent for micro-SPE of preconcentration of triazole fungicide residues before their quantitation by HPLC. The efficient material is in-situ synthesized after heating of citric acid in a single-step. The utilization of melamine sponges could eliminate the centrifugation step for phase separation. Various extraction factors were evaluated by univariate experimental design. Under the optimized fabrication factors and micro-SPE conditions, this as-prepared material was evaluated for the extraction and enrichment of triazole fungicide residues from edible fungi samples. Afterwards, the greenness of the investigated approach was assessed by two metric tools including, Analytical Eco-scale and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) method.

Whole the required chemicals and reagents in the experiment were of analytical grade and used as such without any further treatment. Five triazole standards with a high purity of ≥ 99 %, including myclobutanil, triadimefon, tebuconazole, hexaconazole and diniconazole were supplied by Dr. Ehren-storfer GmbH (Germany). Each compound’s standard stock solutions (1000 mg L−1) was prepared in methanol and stored at 4℃. Working solutions were diluted standard solution daily with water. HPLC-grade of acetonitrile, ethanol and methanol were supplied from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). n-Hexane was brought from Leonid quality our forte (Bangalore, India). Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from KemAus (Australia). For all the experiments, deionized water with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm was obtained from Millipore Waters, USA. Melamine sponges (cosmetic grade) were purchased from an online vendor.

Five triazoles were separated and quantified using a Waters 1525 Binary HPLC pump (Water, Massachusetts, USA) equipped with a diode array detector (DAD). The acquisition and processing of the chromatographic data was carried out using Empower 3 software. The HPLC setting were as follow: a Purospher® STAR RP-18 endcapped (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 µm) column (Merck, Germany). Triazoles were analyzed under isocratic mobile phase of 50 %(v/v) acetonitrile in water at ambient temperature with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 and the injection volume was 20 μL. The TFs were monitored at 220 nm.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Bruker Invenio-S FT-IR (Bruker corp, Massachusetts, USA) were acquired between 4000 and 400 cm− 1 using a diamond lens attenuated total resistance (ATR) approach. Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (SEM; TESCAN Model MIRA, UK) were used to test the morphology of the sorbent. X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker D8 advance, Germany) was used to characterize the crystal structure of the material. Adsorption-desorption isotherms of N2 at 77 K were measured using a Brunner-Emmett-Teller (BET) (Micromeritics® TriStar II Plus (Georgia, USA)). Hot air oven (UN55, Memmert, Germany) was used. An ultrasonic cleaner (Elma, Germany) and vortex mixer (Fisher Scientific, USA) were also used for mixing the solution. A pH meter model AB33PH (Ohaus, USA) was used.

Hydrophobicity was assessed by measuring the contact angle of a water droplet on the geopolymer surface, both modified and unmodified, using a drop shape analyzer (DataPhysics − OCA − Optical contact angle, DataPhysics – Insruments GmbH, Germany). A droplet volume of 3 μL was used, and three measurements were taken to obtain an average value.
The thermal stabilities of the material were investigated by TGA 4000 Thermogravimetric Analyzer (PerkinElmer, Inc., MA, USA).
Vacuum manifold for solid phase extraction procedure (Merck KGaA, Germany) and Millipore SigmaTM pump (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were also used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of hydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A melamine sponge (MeS) was firstly cut into cubes of 1 × 1 × 1 cm3, washed with DI water and ethanol and then left to dry. Subsequently, MeS cube was immersed in an ethanolic solution containing citric acid 0.04 M. The immersing / drying step was repeated until all the solution was soaked up. After that, MeS was immersed in citric acid overnight and then heated to remove solvent at 250 °C for 5 h. Finally, functionalized melamine sponges were characterized and kept in a desiccator until use.

Six edible fungi samples were obtained from local market in Kalasin province, Northeast, Thailand. These samples were washed with tab water and rinsed with deionized water to remove substrates and foreign matter. After that, the edible fungi samples were blended using a mixer-grinder. To extract the target analytes, 2.0 g of sample was mixed with 2.00 mL of acetonitrile. The mixture was vortex for 30 sec and centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and diluted to 10.0 mL with water before it was subjected to the micro-SPE process.

The synthesis material was placed into a syringe (5.00 mL) on cotton fabric, used as SPE cartridge. After that, a representative aliquot of 10 mL of sample solution (from Section 2.4) was loaded into the SPE cartridge by vacuum manifold. After that, the SPE cartridge was washed with 1.0 mL of n-hexane to remove interferences. Following this, the sorbent was eluted with 200 μL of acetonitrile. After filtration through a 0.45 µm nylon syringe filter, 20 μL of eluate were analyzed by HPLC-DAD. The synthesis and micro-SPE application of melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots was shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of superhydrophobic melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots were characterized by FE-SEM, XRD, FTIR, nitrogen adsorption techniques and contact angle of a water droplet. The prepared melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots was characterized by FE-SEM analysis (as shown in Fig. 2 ). The results show that, where the bare MeS (as shown in Fig. 2(a)) had an interconnected 3D framework with large pores size ranging between 100 μm and 200 μm and the surface of framework was very smooth [17]. Due to the large pores size of this material, it could be induced the absorption capacity. After coating treatment-functionalization, the color of MeS changed from white to dark brown revealing the successful coating of carbon dot onto MeS due to the presence of carbon dot that was used in the modification. Fig. 2(b) shows the FE-SEM images of the decoration of carbon dot embedded melamine sponge, respectively. It was found that the MeS was thicker which proved that carbon dot was successfully coated on MeS. Fig. 2(c) shows carbon dot embedded melamine sponge adsorbing triazole fungicides observed with a thicker sponge structure and a thin film-like coating between the sponge pores which shown that the successful adsorption of the target material. Fig. 2(d) shows some pore damage, and the thin coating has disappeared. Since the targets are eluted by suitable elution solvents in the elution step.

The crystal structure of as-prepared synthesis sorbent is determined from XRD patterns as presented in Fig. 3 . There is a board diffraction peak centered at ca 26.5° (2θ) due to highly disordered carbon atoms. Reflexes (100) and (101), for which 2θ ≈ 42.4 and 44.6°, respectively, determine the longitudinal dimension La of the structural elements. The results show that reflexes in the diffraction pattern are asymmetric. That auxiliary indicates the possession of many carbon phases (substructures) with different degrees of order and structural characteristics [25].

The FTIR spectra presented in Fig. 4 showed the characteristic peaks of the bare MeS (as shown in Fig. 4(a)) and the carbon dot embeded MeS (as shown in Fig. 4(b)). It was found that the carbon dot embeded MeS provide various absorption core, almost all are linked to the functional groups. The IR spectrum of the bare MeS, the peaks at 3727.44, 3703.90, 3626.68, 3599.09 and 3331.97 cm−1 represent the characteristic absorption peak of –OH stretching vibration in H2O molecules, inner surface –OH, –OH covalent bond absorption at high frequencies and primary amine respectively, 1635.83 and 1580.12 cm−1 are ascribed to the absorption peak of triazinyl. It is apparent that the peak of 809.97 cm−1 occur in total these three IR spectrums which is the characteristic peak of triazinyl [26,27]. After thermal treatment, the carbon dot embedded melamine sponge composites consisted of these bands confirmed that melamine and formaldehyde are successfully cross-linked with carbon dot (as shown in Fig. 4(c)). The band of 2900.93 and 2971.60 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H. And the new peak at about 1327.12 cm−1 was related to the vibration of methane. For the spectra of bare MeS, the peak at 1079.88 cm−1 was attributed to the structure of the formation of C-O-C. Then, after aging this peak disappeared which confirmed the theoretical prediction.

The pore size of the bare MeS and MeS after modification was determined N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K. The capacity of the materials was investigated in term of surface area and porosity of the prepared materials. BET isotherm without modification and with modification are shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. After modification, MS showed Type-III adsorption in the IUPAC classification (as shown in Fig. 5), illustrating that MS has a large pore size with weak interaction. The surface area of MS was up to 13.3773 m2 g –1 and pore diameter 85.298 Å. After modification, BET isotherm exhibited a reversible type I isotherm which confirmed that the prepared materials provide the low micropores (≈1 nm). Because of the low micropores could be associated with the MeS supports preparation process. The lower porosity of carbon dot-MS results from the uneven distribution of the carbon dot on the MS surface. The result indicated that carbon dot obstructed the pores of MS, resulting in a preconcentration of triazoles. The mechanism of carbon dot embedded melamine sponge with studied compounds were occurred. The BET surface area and pore parameter (Vpore) of the as-prepared sorbent material were –23.5367 m2/g and 0.001 cm3/g, respectively. The results showed that the negative surface area and less pore size distribution due to the pores of MeS was filled with carbon dot as a result decreased in the surface area and pore size. The mechanism of carbon dot embedded melamine sponge to exhibit a hydrophobic behavior due to the conversion of hydroxyl groups to C–O–C in the CA-CD-MeS, which is illustrated in Fig. 6 . Two reactions occurred as follows (i) direct hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of citric acid precursor and the secondary amine groups on the melamine sponge skeleton’s surface and (ii) citric acid-carbon dot-melamine sponge (CA-CD-MeS) binds to the target analytes at the active site via electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding, respectively. Finally, resulted in the self-assembled multilayer covalently bonded onto the surface of MeS after modification.

To characterize the hydrophobicity of the modified and unmodified MeS, the contact angle of a water droplet on their surfaces was measured (Fig. 7 ). Based on the measured value of the water contact angle, θ, surfaces can be classified as superhydrophilic (θ ∼ 0◦), hydrophilic (θ = 30◦ ∼ 90◦), hydrophobic (θ = 90◦∼ 150◦) and superhydrophobic (θ > 150◦) [28]. For the modified MeS surface, the average water contact angle was approximately 124◦, which is classified as hydrophobic (i.e., the water contact angle ≥ 90◦∼ 150◦). In contrast, the water contact angle of the unmodified MeS surface could not be recorded because the water droplet was immediately absorbed by the material, indicating a highly hydrophilic nature. Thus, also this result confirms the successful introduction of the hydrophobic alkyl chains of the carbon dot coupling agent to the MeS surface.

In addition, the thermal stabilities of the material which indicates the stability of material under the thermal condition [29], were investigated by thermogravimetric (TGA) analysis. TGA curves of bare melamine sponge (MeS) and carbon dot embedded melamine sponge (MeS-CD) are shown in Fig. 8 . Three weight losses of bare MeS (as shown in Fig. 8(a)) was shown at the following temperatures: 30–150, 350–410, and 410–650 °C. In the 30–150 °C range, the mass loss is derived from the evaporation of the water absorbed in the MeS [30,31]. The weight of bare MeS is stable before 350 °C, confirming the stability of the sponges. A rapid weight loss in MeS was observed from 410 to 700 °C. The breakdown of the methylene bridge (HN–CH2–NH) of the MeS structure contributes to the mass loss between 350 and 410 °C. At the temperature higher than 410 °C, the mass loss was attributed to the thermal decomposition of the triazine ring [32]. At 650 °C, less than 20 % weight is left. A slight mass loss can also be observed by increasing the temperature above 650 °C, indicating still N, H, and C releasing at higher temperatures. The thermal decomposition of the MeS-CD (as shown in Fig. 8(b)) mainly occurred in the temperature range of 122 to 400 °C, which is consistent with the thermal behavior of the reported typical critic acid [33]. The initial weight increases results from water vapor adsorption by the hydrophilic functional groups of citric acid [34]. The slight drop to 15–20 wt% which occurred from 120 °C until 225 °C, can be attributed to the citric acid as a carbon dot precursor. No significant weight loss was observed in both before 500 °C, due to the pre-carbonization processes. Thus, the MeS and MeS-CD show thermal stability.

Ideally, a good sorbent should be able to function in acidic, alkaline, and neutral solutions [35]. For the aforementioned reason, the stability of as-prepared melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots under these conditions was evaluated. The sorbent was immersed in acidic (pH of 3), alkaline (pH of 10), and neutral (pH of 7) solutions for 24 h, then washed and dried. After that, the as-prepared sorbent was then extracted of triazole fungicides prior to HPLC analysis. The result was shown in Fig. 9 . It was found that the peak areas were slightly different but the variation was not statistically significant after immersed at three different solution. The high chemical stability of the sponge could be a consequence of the cross-linked network and chemical inertness of carbon dots grafted on its surface.

Various factors influencing the extraction efficiencies of the micro-SPE were studied. All experiments were examined in triplicate using standard solutions of triazoles 100 µg L –1. The synthesis conditions were tested to obtain maximum loading of MeS with citric acid. The principle used to select the optimum synthesis conditions was the total adsorption capability of the resulting carbon dot embedded melamine sponge. However, the adsorption and desorption of each TFs also was considered. Furthermore, 4 sizes of MeS were investigated including 0.3 × 0.3 × 0.3, 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5, 1 × 1 × 1, and 1.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 cm3. As shown in Fig. S1(a), it indicated that the highest extraction recoveries of most analytes were achieved using 1 × 1 × 1 cm3 of MeS, except diniconazole. Single MeS cubes (1 × 1 × 1 cm3) were used as the optimal size to optimize the synthesis unless otherwise stated.

The citric acid (as precursor) was produced at different concentrations of 0.01, 0.02, 0.04 and 0.1 M by dissolving citric acid (monohydrate) powder in ethanol. The effect of the concentration of citric acid on the extraction efficiency of triazole fungicides was evaluated in the range of 0.01 to 0.1 M. It was found that the extraction efficiency in terms of peak area of all triazole fungicides decreased with the increase in the concentration of citric acid up to 0.04 M and decreased when the concentration of citric was higher than 0.04 M. Thus, 0.04 M of citric acid was selected for further experiments (as shown in Fig. S1(b)). After that, MeS cubes were immersed in citric acid solution until they dampened.

Carbon dots are prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid at 200 °C [36]. The heating temperature studied here was less than 280 °C to avoid burning the sponge [23]. The heating temperature was studied between 130 and 280 °C, for 5 h (as shown in Fig. S1(c)). Due to, the melting point of anhydrous citric acid is 153 °C, therefore the extraction efficiency at 130 °C is very low. The extraction efficiency in terms of the peak area of all triazoles increased when the temperature was up to 250 °C. While the temperature increased, the MeS was shrunk and impaired, therefore that the extraction efficiency decreased. Consequently, the temperature was selected at 250 °C.

In order to ensure the extraction efficiency of investigated procedure for the analysis of triazole fungicides, various parameters were studied such as kind and volume of elution solvent. The optimization process was carried out using the univariate method as a one-factor-at-a-time approach with three replicates for each factor. All experiments were examined in triplicate using standard solutions of triazoles 100 µg L –1. Relative standard deviation (%RSD) was utilized to assess experimental precision.

The kinds of elution solvent are an essential parameter for micro-SPE since it determines the accomplish of the analytes elution and therefore will consequence the sensitivity [37]. Due to the polarity of the target analytes, polar solvents were investigated including methanol, acetonitrile (ACN), and deionization water. The result was shown in Fig. S1(d). It was found that the high extraction efficiency of all analytes was obtained when acetonitrile was used due to a wide polarity range. Therefore, acetonitrile was selected as the desorption solvent, studied triazole fungicides obtained an increased extraction efficiency.

The effect of volume of the acetonitrile (as elution solvent) was also studied in the range of 100–250 µL. As shown in Fig. S1(e) illustrate that the maximum recoveries of all TFs were achieved when the volume was 200 µL and then decreased due to dilution effect. Therefore, volume of the acetonitrile of 200 µL was chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and selectivity of the sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: Reusability experiments of the sorbent were studied after desorbing the target analytes. Between the cycles, the sorbent was washed using acetonitrile and then dried for the next micro-SPE cycle. The result was shown in Fig. S1(f). It was found that the peak area of five TFs had significant decrease after 3 micro-SPE cycles, indicating that the as-prepared CA-CD-MeS could be reused at least 3 times (data not shown). The extraction efficiency decreased thereafter because the CA-CD-MeS was destroyed. Consequently, this alternative sorbent was cost-effective for the determination of triazoles.

To estimate the selectivity of CA-CD-MeS to the tested analytes, the extraction efficiency of carbamate pesticides (methomyl, carbofuran, carbaryl, isoprocarb, fenobucarb), and neonicotinoid insecticides (clotianidin, imidacloprid, dinotefuran, thiacloprid) were examined. The extraction was performed in fungi samples. The results showed that only five triazole studied were extracted from CA-CD-MeS from the studied samples. The total extraction recovery of the five triazole studied were in the range of 80.6 – 120.5 %. Therefore, the developed sorbent material exhibited some selectivity over some commonly used pesticides, whereas we see prospect of it being used as a sorbent for some of them.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the analytical figure of merit of the proposed method were evaluated by assessing the linearity, correlation coefficients, the limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), enrichment factor (EF) and extraction recovery (ER). The analytical figure of merit of the developed method are summarized in Table 1 . The method exhibited good linearity in concentration ranges of 9–500 μg L−1 with the correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.99. The LODs and LOQs, which are calculated based on signal-to noise ratios of 3 and 10, respectively, were in the range of 3 – 10 μg L−1 and 9 –30 μg L−1, respectively. The RSDs of the intra (n = 5) and interday (n = 3 × 5) at three concentration levels (30, 50, and 100 μg L−1) were used to evaluate the reproducibility of the proposed methodology. All targets exhibited satisfactory intraday and interday precision, with %RSDs less than 8. The enrichment factor (EF) were found to be in the range of 19.87–64.60 folds when computed as the concentration ratio of the analytes in the settled phase (Cset ) later the sedimented phase is diluted with 150 μL of acetonitrile and in the aqueous sample (Co ). The extraction recovery (ER) were calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
E R
%
=
C f
V f
C i
V i
×
100
where Vi and Vf are the initial and final volumes, Ci is the initial sample solution concentration and Cf is the final concentration of analyte in the desorption solvent [38]. %ER were in the range of 39.74 – 129.20. Fig. S2 demonstrates the chromatograms of the triazole fungicides generated by direct HPLC and after extraction by the investigated micro-SPE-HPLC procedure. The signal was increased after extraction using the proposed micro-SPE-HPLC method.

The robustness of investigated method becomes evaluated to decide their ability to face up to minor changes in access states. The %RSD of peak area and retention time were studied after samples have been injected with some minor change of flow rate (± 0.1 mL min−1), detection wavelength (± 2 nm), and mobile phase composition (± 2 %). The result received from each parameter (n = 9) were also determined by a one-way ANOVA test at a 95 % confidence level (p < 0.05) (data not shown). The result considered acceptable with %RSDs lower than 8.2 and 1.5 for peak area and retention time, respectively. It was found that, no effect on the resolution of the all analytes by adjustment of some separation factors (data not shown). Thus, the proposed method was robustness.

The extraction of triazole residues in fungi samples was accomplished according to the developed micro-SPE-HPLC method. A matrix-matched calibration approach was used to evaluate the matrix effect in real samples. A set of matrix-matched calibration curves was prepared by extracting representative fungi samples spiked with 50–500 μg kg−1 of each target analyte. Most triazoles exhibit wide calibration capability and excellent linearity, with R2 greater than 0.99 for all samples. The matrix effect (ME) was calculated by comparing the ratio of the slopes of the matrix-matched curve to that of the solvent (Eq. (2))
(2)
M E
%
=
(
S m
/
S S
)
× 100
where Sm and Ss are the slopes of the calibration curve in the matrix and solvent, respectively. In general, there are no matrix effects in case of a ME between 80 and 120 %, minor matrix effects in case of a ME between 50 and 80 % or 120–150 %, and major matrix effects in case of a ME less than 50 % or greater than 150 % [39,40]. As seen in Table S1, minor and major MEs were found in fungi sample studies.

To verify the applicability of the proposed method, six edible fungi samples spiked at two concentrations (30 μg kg−1 and 50 μg kg−1) were analyzed. Results showed that, none of the triazoles were found in the studied edible fungi samples. The typical chromatograms of studied edible fungi sample II, III and IV are demonstrated in Fig. S3. The extraction recoveries of all TFs in the spiked fungi samples extracted under the selected conditions were in a range of 70.45–110.38 %, with RSDs below 5 %. The obtained results were shown in Table S2.

Two metric tools were performed to confirm the greenness of the proposed approach via Analytical Eco-scale [41] and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) [42]. The ideal green analysis according to the Analytical Eco-Scale has a value of 100. Penalty points are assigned for each of the parameters of the analytical procedure that deviate from the ideal green analysis (reagent use, risk, energy and waste). A set of penalty points (PPs) are used to assign the departure from ideal greenness. The PPs for hazardous chemicals can be assessed by multiplying the sub-total PPs for a given quantity and the hazard. The ultimate AEC value can be calculated from “AEC = 100 − total PPs”. If the score is more than 75, the method is expressed as “excellent green” procedure, while values in the range 75 < x < 50 is considered “acceptable green analysis” and values below 50 indicate insufficient green method [43]. The final score for this method is 73 (as shown in Table S3) and it can be acceptable as a green method.

The AGREE metric, assessed via The Analytical Greenness Calculator software, evaluates adherence to the 12 principles of Green Analytical Chemistry, assigning scores from 0 to 1 for each criterion [42]. Results are depicted through a pictogram, indicating a final score from 0 to 1 and color-coded from green to red. The width of the segment corresponding to each individual GAC principle shows its impact on the final score for the method. As depicted in Fig. S4, the AGREE evaluation resulted in a final score of 0.47 which shown that the present method can be acceptable as a green. An overall score greater than 0.6 [41] indicates a high level of adherence to green analytical chemistry principles, such as reducing or eliminating hazardous compounds, reducing waste creation, optimizing energy use, and increasing health and safety. It displays a strong commitment to environmental stewardship and sustainability [44,45]. The lowest performing subsectors was obtained in sectors 3 (device positioning) and 10 (source of reagents). The evaluation, assessed by Analytical Eco-scale and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE), confirmed its superior environmental friendliness.

Table 2 exhibits the analytical performance of the established carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method compared with other microextraction methods [46–51]. With a brief comparison among the methods, it can be seen that the proposed method shows an extraction method, analytical technique, and relative recovery percentage. Also, LODs values are comparable with the other methods. The important point of this method is introducing an alternative sorbent which is based on the carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method, easy and quick operation. Compared with the traditional SPE, the present extraction method is considerably simpler as it does not need additional centrifugation steps or external magnets, and the carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method used in the present method can be quickly separated from the sample solution. In addition, the volumes of the organic solvents and the operating times required for the carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method as a sorbent in the micro-SPE method were significantly lower than those required for other reported methods. It was found that a simple and efficient propose method could be determined trace triazole in edible fungi samples. Moreover, the favorable analytical figures of merit and cost effective, not only its potential for reuse, but also renders it a better alternative for matrix purification.

Fabrication of superhydrophobic melamine sponge functionalized with carbon dots was investigated and used as an effective adsorbent for the extraction of triazole fungicides. Carbon dot (CD) embedded melamine sponge method was successfully prepared by a simple and facile method by using citric acid (CA) as a precursor. CA-CD was used not only as an adsorption assistant but also as an adhesive in the preparation of functionalized sponge materials. Melamine sponge was selected as an adsorbed base material in the micro-SPE to simplify the separation procedures with superficial one-step infiltration, excellent mechanical stability, and low cost. This study provides a new avenue for exploring other functionalized coating adsorbent materials using CA-CD as a coater and for preparing new functionalized sponges to detect other contaminants in different complex matrix samples. The carbon dot embedded melamine sponge method as a sorbent in micro-SPE assessment was established for analysis of trace level contamination of triazole fungicides in fungi samples followed by HPLC detection. It is conceivable that CA-CD can be operative not only in case of triazoles but also for other analytes which can interact with carbon dot. This developed approach, with its green procedure, which could be enhanced by performing in-situ strategy, eradicating the utilize of hazardous reagents, and replacing them with their green alternatives.

Rawikan Kachangoon: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yanawath Santaladchaiyakit: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Orrasa Prasitnok: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jitlada Vichapong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
